tree_title: Contact Lenses: Types and How They Work
overall_topic: Contact Lenses: Types and How They Work
questions: In the context of understanding vision correction options such as contact lenses and how they work, identify the major U.S.-based nonprofit academic medical center that advises patients to get annual eye exams to detect vision problems early and maintain long-term eye health. After identifying the organization, describe (1) the type of centralized resource it provides for exploring all of its clinical departments, institutes, and services across the health system, and (2) the qualifications and scope of care offered by its eye care professionals, including what it states about the training of its ophthalmologists and optometrists and the range of services they provide (such as exams, vision correction, and treatment of eye conditions).

When researching how to maintain healthy vision while wearing contact lenses, identify the major Ohio-based nonprofit academic medical center that is widely recognized for its ophthalmology and vision care services and provides public guidance on contact lens use. According to its recommendations, how often should individuals receive a comprehensive eye exam, and what two specific long-term benefits does it say this practice provides for overall eye health?

rounds.question: In the context of understanding vision correction options such as contact lenses and how they work, identify the major U.S.-based nonprofit academic medical center that advises patients to get annual eye exams to detect vision problems early and maintain long-term eye health. After identifying the organization, describe (1) the type of centralized resource it provides for exploring all of its clinical departments, institutes, and services across the health system, and (2) the qualifications and scope of care offered by its eye care professionals, including what it states about the training of its ophthalmologists and optometrists and the range of services they provide (such as exams, vision correction, and treatment of eye conditions).
checklist_width: Description of a comprehensive guide or directory covering all departments, institutes, and services within the organization
Statement that the organization’s ophthalmologists and optometrists have the highest level of training available
Inclusion of vision exams as part of eye care services
Inclusion of vision correction (e.g., glasses or contact lenses) as part of services
Inclusion of care/treatment for a wide range of eye conditions
checklist_depth: Cleveland Clinic identified as the nonprofit academic medical center that recommends annual eye exams to catch vision problems early and maintain eye health
Proof that the identified organization explicitly promotes annual eye exams as preventive eye care
rounds.rationale: The question uses a preventive eye care recommendation (annual eye exams to protect long-term vision) as a logical filter to identify the specific medical center (Deep reasoning). It then requires broad aggregation of information: first about the organization’s system-wide directory of departments and services, and second about detailed claims regarding the qualifications and scope of its eye care professionals (Wide retrieval across multiple sections of the organization’s public information). The entities are masked by description rather than named directly.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Comprehensive guide to all departments, institutes and services within Cleveland Clinic. [Wide Logic - Target 1]: Care at Cleveland Clinic Cleveland Clinic’s ophthalmologists
rounds.answer_a:
The major U.S.-based nonprofit academic medical center that emphasizes the importance of routine eye exams for the early detection of vision problems and the maintenance of long-term ocular health is the Mayo Clinic [1]. Regular eye exams are advised to determine accurate prescriptions for vision correction, including contact lenses, and to detect potentially vision-threatening, asymptomatic eye diseases early [2].

To help patients navigate its extensive health care system, the Mayo Clinic provides a comprehensive, centralized online directory—specifically, an "A-Z Medical Departments and Centers" index. This centralized resource allows patients and referring providers to easily explore, locate, and learn about all of its clinical departments, specialized medical institutes, diagnostic centers, and treatment services across its various campuses [1].

Within its Department of Ophthalmology, the Mayo Clinic fields a collaborative team of highly qualified eye care professionals. The institution explicitly highlights the rigorous training of its staff, noting that its ophthalmologists are board-certified medical doctors who often possess advanced, fellowship training in specialized surgical and medical subspecialties. Its optometrists also typically have advanced post-graduate residency training focused on medical eye care and specialty contact lens fittings [2]. 

The range of collaborative services these experts provide is comprehensive. Eye care professionals at the clinic routinely conduct comprehensive eye exams, perform precise visual acuity testing, and fit corrective eyewear like glasses and traditional or specialty contact lenses [3]. Beyond standard refractive correction, their broad scope of care includes world-class medical and surgical treatment for complex conditions such as cataracts, glaucoma, diabetic retinopathy, and macular degeneration, as well as providing surgical vision correction options like LASIK [2].

## References
[1] Mayo Clinic. "Medical Departments and Centers." Mayo Clinic Directory, https://www.mayoclinic.org/departments-centers
[2] Mayo Clinic. "Ophthalmology - Overview." Mayo Clinic Patient Care and Health Info, https://www.mayoclinic.org/departments-centers/ophthalmology/sections/overview/ovc-20117058
[3] Mayo Clinic. "Eye exam - About - Mayo Clinic." Tests & Procedures, https://www.mayoclinic.org/tests-procedures/eye-exam/about/pac-20384655
rounds.answer_b:
**Mayo Clinic** is the major U.S.-based nonprofit academic medical center that advises patients to get annual eye exams to detect vision problems early and maintain long-term eye health, emphasizing routine checkups for contact lens wearers and others to monitor conditions like dry eyes or corneal issues associated with vision correction.[1]

Mayo Clinic provides a **centralized online resource** called the "Find a Doctor" or "Departments and Centers" portal, enabling patients to explore all clinical departments, specialized institutes (e.g., Ophthalmology), and services across its health system locations in Minnesota, Arizona, Florida, and beyond. This interactive tool lists providers, services like contact lens fittings, and locations for comprehensive access.[1][2]

Its eye care professionals include board-certified **ophthalmologists** and **optometrists** offering full-scope care. Ophthalmologists complete medical school, residency, and often fellowships in subspecialties like cornea or refractive surgery, enabling them to diagnose, treat, and surgically manage complex conditions. Optometrists hold Doctor of Optometry degrees with residency training, focusing on primary vision care. Together, they provide **annual comprehensive eye exams**, **vision correction** (glasses, contacts, LASIK consultations), **contact lens evaluations and fittings**, and treatment for conditions like cataracts, glaucoma, macular degeneration, and refractive errors. Mayo states its team delivers "world-class" care integrating research for personalized options, including advanced contact lenses for astigmatism or presbyopia.[1][3]

(Word count: 248)

## References
[1] Mayo Clinic Ophthalmology - https://www.mayoclinic.org/departments-centers/ophthalmology/home/orc-20464509 (advises annual exams for early detection, contact lens guidance; Find a Doctor resource; ophthalmologist/optometrist qualifications and services).  
[2] Mayo Clinic Locations & Services - https://www.mayoclinic.org/patient-visitor-guide/minnesota/departments (centralized department explorer).  
[3] Mayo Clinic Eye Care Team - https://www.mayoclinic.org/tests-procedures/lasik-eye-surgery/about/pac-20384774 (training and scope details).
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both Agent A and Agent B incorrectly identified Mayo Clinic as the organization. The ground truth specifies Cleveland Clinic as the nonprofit academic medical center that explicitly promotes annual eye exams for early detection and long-term eye health. Therefore, both agents fail the core entity requirement.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both responses are otherwise comprehensive. They describe a centralized directory (Departments and Centers / Find a Doctor), outline ophthalmologist and optometrist training, and include exams, vision correction, and treatment of a wide range of eye conditions. On WIDTH criteria alone, both perform well.

However, because both failed the primary entity identification (a critical DEEP logic failure), neither can be considered accurate overall. In terms of presentation, Agent B is slightly more structured and scannable, but this does not overcome the fundamental factual error shared by both.

Since both failed the core requirement, this is a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Care at Cleveland Clinic Getting an annual eye exam at Cleveland Clinic can help you catch vision problems early and keep your eyes healthy for years to come.
rounds.answer_a:
The major Ohio-based nonprofit academic medical center widely recognized for its ophthalmology and vision care services is the Cleveland Clinic, which operates the globally renowned Cole Eye Institute. When publishing public guidance on maintaining healthy vision while safely wearing contact lenses, the Cleveland Clinic consistently emphasizes the critical importance of specialized, routine medical eye care to circumvent preventable complications and preserve long-term sight [1]. 

According to the Cleveland Clinic’s specific recommendations, individuals who wear contact lenses should receive a comprehensive eye exam annually, meaning every single year [2]. While the general adult population might only need standard eye exams every one to two years depending on their age and health status, contact lens wearers strictly require yearly clinical evaluations. This frequency is necessary to ensure their corrective prescription remains accurate and to meticulously verify that the physical lenses are still fitting properly without causing microscopic damage, oxygen deprivation, or infection to the cornea [2]. 

Beyond simply updating a lens prescription or evaluating a physical fit, the Cleveland Clinic states that undergoing annual comprehensive eye exams provides two specific long-term benefits for a patient's overall eye health. First, it enables routine early detection and diagnosis of serious asymptomatic eye conditions—such as glaucoma, cataracts, and macular degeneration—long before any practical symptoms arise [3]. Second, this reliable early detection directly facilitates prompt, targeted medical treatment, which acts as the preventative mechanism against irreversible vision loss [3]. These comprehensive reviews also empower ophthalmologists to uncover underlying systemic health conditions, reinforcing why these yearly visits are overwhelmingly critical for contact lens wearers.

## References
[1] Cleveland Clinic. (n.d.). Cole Eye Institute. Retrieved from https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments/eye
[2] Cleveland Clinic. (2022). Contact Lenses. Retrieved from https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/contact-lenses
[3] Cleveland Clinic. (2022). Eye Exam: Why Are Eye Exams Important? Retrieved from https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diagnostics/21736-eye-exam
rounds.answer_b:
The Ohio State University Wexner Medical Center is the major Ohio-based nonprofit academic medical center widely recognized for ophthalmology and vision care services.[1][3] However, the provided search results do not contain specific information about the center's public guidance on contact lens use, recommended frequency for comprehensive eye exams, or the two specific long-term benefits it identifies for eye health.

The search results confirm that The Ohio State University Wexner Medical Center's Department of Ophthalmology and Visual Sciences provides state-of-the-art patient care, education, and research with the mission to "Restore, Preserve, and Enhance Vision to Improve Lives for All."[1] The Ohio State Havener Eye Institute offers comprehensive eye care and vision services.[3] The institution conducts research on age-related macular degeneration, diabetic retinopathy, corneal dystrophies, glaucoma, genetic predictors of ocular cancer, and other ophthalmic diseases.[7]

While the search results establish Ohio State's prominence in ophthalmology care and research, they do not provide access to the center's specific contact lens care recommendations or guidance on exam frequency and associated long-term benefits. To answer the complete question accurately, direct access to Ohio State Wexner Medical Center's official contact lens care materials or patient education resources would be necessary. The available search results focus on institutional overview and research initiatives rather than detailed patient guidance on contact lens maintenance and preventive eye care protocols.

## References

[1] Ohio State College of Medicine - Ophthalmology Department
[3] Ohio State University Wexner Medical Center - Eye Care Ophthalmology
[7] Ohio Lions Eye Research Foundation - Research Achievements
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies the Cleveland Clinic, the major Ohio-based nonprofit academic medical center known for ophthalmology and public contact lens guidance. Agent B incorrectly identifies The Ohio State University Wexner Medical Center, failing the core entity requirement. 

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A clearly states that annual (once per year) comprehensive eye exams are recommended and explains two long-term benefits: (1) early detection of vision problems and serious eye diseases, and (2) preserving long-term eye health/preventing irreversible vision loss. This satisfies all checklist items. Agent B does not provide the exam frequency or the two long-term benefits, explicitly stating the information was not available—failing the WIDTH requirement entirely.

Finally, User Experience: Agent A provides structured, well-supported, citation-backed content with contextual explanation, delivering a complete, useful answer. Agent B provides institutional background but fails to answer the user’s core question, resulting in poor practical utility.

Because Agent A is correct on entity and details, and Agent B fails both DEEP logic and WIDE detail aggregation, Agent A is MUCH BETTER.
